source_paper_title: Popular Maximum-Utility Matchings with Matroid Constraints
context:
Page 6, Section 1.2 (Hardness results discussion leading to Theorem 4); reiterated Page 22, Section 5.1 (One-sided Preferences Model)

For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.
title: Deterministic polynomial-time solvability of the Exact Matching problem in bipartite graphs
latex:
Given a bipartite graph \(G=(A\cup B,E)\) with \(|A|=|B|\), suppose each edge \(e\in E\) is colored either red or blue. For an integer \(k\in\{0,1,\dots,|A|\}\), define the decision problem:
\[
\text{\bf Exact-Matching}(G,k):\ \text{Does there exist a perfect matching }M\subseteq E\text{ such that }|\{e\in M: e\text{ is red}\}|=k?
\]
The problem is to design a deterministic algorithm that, for every input \((G,k)\), decides \(\text{Exact-Matching}(G,k)\) in time polynomial in \(|A|+|B|+|E|\).
